chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ The leaves are the specific part of the plant noted to contain hydrocyanic acid. While the plant is generally considered mild to moderately toxic, ingestion of the leaves can cause significant respiratory signs.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ This compound is a potent cellular toxin. It interferes with cellular respiration, potentially leading to labored breathing and respiratory distress. 4. Notes on
chemicals.name: Hydrocyanic Acid (also known as Hydrogen Cyanide or Prussic Acid)

chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ The flower seeds contain these toxic tannins. The seeds and fruits are often cited as the most toxic parts of the plant due to these compounds.Additional Clinical Note: Ingestion of the flowers or seeds can also specifically cause eye discharge in cats, alongside the gastrointestinal and respiratory symptoms mentioned above.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Tannins act as irritants to the gastrointestinal tract. Ingestion typically leads to digestive issues, including vomiting and diarrhea. 4. Notes on
chemicals.name: Tannins

description: Bird of Paradise (Strelitzia reginae and Strelitzia nicolai) is a popular ornamental plant known for its dramatic orange-and-blue flowers and large tropical foliage. It is mildly toxic to cats. The primary hazards are the seeds and seed pods, which contain tannins and small amounts of hydrocyanic acid (cyanide-releasing compounds), along with other irritants in the plant's sap. While the toxicity level is low compared to many houseplants, cats are more sensitive than humans to these compounds, and ingestion of flowers or seeds can cause consistent gastrointestinal symptoms.
name: Bird of Paradise
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, mild diarrhea, and drowsiness are the most common signs; symptoms are typically self-limiting but unpleasant.
Seeds and pods are the highest-risk parts: The striking seed pods are the most dangerous component and should be removed promptly if accessible.
Action: Monitor the cat and contact a vet if vomiting is persistent, or if the cat appears unusually lethargic after known or suspected ingestion.

symptoms.name: Nausea, Vomiting, and Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: These digestive issues are primarily caused by tannins found in the flower seeds and fruits, which act as irritants.Labored Breathing

symptoms.name: Labored Breathing (Respiratory Distress)
symptoms.notes: This symptom is linked to the ingestion of the leaves, which contain hydrocyanic acid (prussic acid).Eye Discharge

symptoms.name: Eye Discharge
symptoms.notes: This is a specific clinical sign associated with Bird of Paradise toxicity noted in multiple veterinary sources.Drowsiness

symptoms.name: Drowsiness (Lethargy)
symptoms.notes: This symptom is primarily associated with the ingestion of the seeds or fruit.

treatments.description: If the ingestion was recent, the veterinarian may administer medication to induce vomiting (emesis) to expel the plant material from the stomach before toxins are fully absorbed.
treatments.name: Decontamination (Inducing Vomiting)
treatments.notes: This is a general protocol for plant poisoning to prevent further absorption of toxins. Sources emphasize that pet owners should not attempt to induce vomiting at home (e.g., using hydrogen peroxide) as it can cause injury; this must be done by a veterinary professional.

treatments.description: The administration of intravenous (IV) fluids to maintain hydration and electrolyte balance.
treatments.name: Fluid Therapy
treatments.notes: This is necessary to counteract dehydration caused by the gastrointestinal symptoms associated with Bird of Paradise ingestion, specifically vomiting and diarrhea. Fluid therapy also helps flush toxins from the body and supports kidney function.

treatments.description: The use of medications to control nausea and close observation of vital signs, particularly respiratory effort.
treatments.name: Symptomatic Management and Monitoring
treatments.notes: Because the leaves contain hydrocyanic acid (prussic acid), veterinarians will monitor for and treat labored breathing. Additionally, since the plant is known to cause specific eye discharge and gastrointestinal irritation (from tannins), care may include eye treatments or gastroprotectants to sooth
